q: Summarize the book of Isaiah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Isaiah son of Amoz, prophesying in Judah c. 740–680 B.C.; the book is the work of this single prophet, who ministered across the reigns of Uzziah through Hezekiah.

**Genre.** Prophecy (with poetic oracles).

**Theme.** The LORD is the Holy One of Israel who judges sin yet saves a remnant; salvation belongs to Him alone, accomplished through a suffering Servant and a coming Davidic King.

**Outline.**
- 1–12 — Judgment and hope for Judah; the promised Immanuel and Davidic King
- 13–27 — Oracles against the nations and apocalyptic judgment
- 28–35 — Woes and the future glory of Zion
- 36–39 — Historical interlude: Hezekiah and Assyria
- 40–55 — Comfort, the Servant Songs, and redemption
- 56–66 — The new heavens and new earth

**Christ & significance.** The virgin-born Immanuel (7:14) and the Suffering Servant who bears our iniquities (52:13–53:12) are fulfilled in Jesus, making Isaiah a cornerstone of New Testament gospel proclamation.
summary: Isaiah son of Amoz, c. 740–680 B.C. Judgment on Judah and the nations gives way to comfort, the Servant Songs, and the new creation, centered on the Holy One of Israel.

q: Summarize the book of Jeremiah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Jeremiah, the priest-prophet of Anathoth, with his scribe Baruch; ministering c. 627–586 B.C. and beyond, from Josiah's reign through the fall of Jerusalem.

**Genre.** Prophecy (oracles, narrative, and laments).

**Theme.** Persistent covenant unfaithfulness brings God's judgment through Babylon, yet the LORD pledges restoration and a new covenant written on the heart.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Call and commission of Jeremiah
- 2–25 — Oracles of judgment against Judah and Jerusalem
- 26–29 — Conflict with false prophets and leaders
- 30–33 — The Book of Consolation: restoration and the new covenant
- 34–45 — Jerusalem's fall and its aftermath
- 46–52 — Oracles against the nations; the city's destruction

**Christ & significance.** Jeremiah's promised new covenant (31:31–34) is inaugurated by Christ's blood, securing forgiveness and the law written within, as the New Testament declares.
summary: Jeremiah of Anathoth with Baruch, c. 627–586 B.C. The weeping prophet warns of Babylonian judgment for covenant breaking, yet promises a new covenant written on the heart.

q: Summarize the book of Lamentations: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally Jeremiah; composed shortly after the destruction of Jerusalem, c. 586 B.C.

**Genre.** Lament (acrostic poetry).

**Theme.** Grief over the fall of Jerusalem under God's righteous judgment, voiced honestly to the LORD, yet anchored in the steadfast hope of His mercies.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Jerusalem desolate and weeping
- 2 — The LORD's anger against Zion
- 3 — Personal anguish and renewed hope in God's mercy
- 4 — The horrors of the siege
- 5 — A communal prayer for restoration

**Christ & significance.** Its confession that God's mercies are new every morning (3:22–23) points to the faithful covenant love ultimately revealed in Christ, the Man of Sorrows who bears His people's grief.
summary: Jeremiah, c. 586 B.C. Five acrostic poems mourn fallen Jerusalem under God's judgment, yet rest in the truth that His steadfast mercies never end.

q: Summarize the book of Ezekiel: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Ezekiel, a priest exiled to Babylon, prophesying c. 593–571 B.C. among the exiles by the Chebar canal.

**Genre.** Prophecy (visions and symbolic acts).

**Theme.** The glory of the sovereign LORD departs from a defiled temple in judgment but returns in restoration, so that the nations and Israel will know that He is the LORD.

**Outline.**
- 1–3 — Ezekiel's vision of God's glory and his call
- 4–24 — Judgment oracles against Jerusalem and its sin
- 25–32 — Oracles against the surrounding nations
- 33–39 — Restoration: new heart, dry bones, and the defeat of Gog
- 40–48 — The vision of the new temple and the LORD's returning glory

**Christ & significance.** God's promise to give a new heart and put His Spirit within His people (36:26–27) is fulfilled in Christ, the true Shepherd (34) whose Spirit brings dead bones to life.
summary: Ezekiel the exiled priest, c. 593–571 B.C. Through visions and signs he announces the departure and return of God's glory, the promise of a new heart, and a new temple.

q: Summarize the book of Daniel: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Daniel, a Judean exile serving in the Babylonian and Persian courts; written in the sixth century B.C., c. 605–530 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative and Apocalyptic.

**Theme.** The Most High God is sovereign over the rise and fall of all earthly kingdoms and will establish His everlasting kingdom, calling His people to faithfulness under pagan rule.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Daniel and his friends in Babylon
- 2–4 — Nebuchadnezzar's dreams and God's sovereignty
- 5 — Belshazzar's feast and Babylon's fall
- 6 — Daniel in the lions' den under Persia
- 7–12 — Apocalyptic visions of kingdoms, the Son of Man, and the end

**Christ & significance.** Daniel's vision of one like a Son of Man receiving an everlasting dominion (7:13–14) is claimed by Jesus as His own title, and the indestructible stone kingdom (2:44) is fulfilled in Christ's reign.
summary: Daniel, a sixth-century B.C. exile (c. 605–530 B.C.). Court narratives and apocalyptic visions display God's sovereignty over the nations and the everlasting kingdom of the Son of Man.
